segments.s375-g65: وزان is an archaic contraction of وز آن / از آن meaning “and/of those” (prepositional phrase introducing the group). دیگران is the plural noun “the others.” کشته is the past participle used adjectivally/predicatively “killed, slain” describing those others. بد here functions adverbially/intensifying the sense of the killing (i.e. badly/terribly slain) and attaches directly to کشته; the numeral that follows gives the count.
segments.s375-g66: This segment is the numeral phrase plus the descriptive noun: هزار و صد و شست و سه is a classical numeric string meaning “a hundred thousand and one hundred and sixty‑three” (here written with an archaic form شست for شصت). و is the coordinating conjunction “and” that links the parts of the number. نامدار is the adjective/noun “renowned, illustrious” qualifying the counted people.
segments.s375-g67: ز is the poetic preposition از “from/of.” خسته is used substantively here (“the exhausted” or “the spent ones”). بدی qualifies خسته and means “of the bad/foes” or “of the evildoers” (a genitive/qualifying noun). The following numeric phrase سه هزار و دویست gives the count “three thousand two hundred,” linked internally by و.
segments.s375-g68: برین is composed of بر “on/upon” + ین (an archaic orthographic variant of این) giving “on this.” جای “place/spot” names the location. The second بر repeats the preposition (idiomatic reinforcement: “upon this very place”). تا توانی is an expression “as far as you can / as strength permits,” with توانی the present‑tense ability form “you can.” مه is an emphatic/quantifying adjective here meaning “great/considerable,” and ایست is an archaic copula/verb meaning “is/remains” (so together: “it is many / they remain”).
segments.s375-g33: چو here is the temporal conjunction 'when'. اندر + شکست = a locative/time phrase 'in the rout/defeat'; آن شب تیره‌گون is a definite noun phrase 'that darkened night' (آن = 'that', شب = 'night', تیره‌گون = postpositive adjective 'dark-hued/gloomy'). The whole segment sets the time/scene for the following action.
segments.s375-g34: به + دشت و بیابان gives the direction/location 'into the plain and wilderness'; فرو خورد is a compound verbal idea 'fell down/poured down' (فرو = downwards, خورد here = befall/fall); خون is the affected noun 'blood'. Word order places the location before the verb for poetic effect.
segments.s375-g35: کی here functions as the proper name/title 'Ki' (a Kayanian king) rather than an interrogative; نامور is an epithet 'renowned'. با سران سپاه is a prepositional phrase 'with the leaders/chiefs of the army' that accompanies the subject.
segments.s375-g36: بیامد is the simple past 'came'. به دیدار functions as 'to (a) sight/meeting' = 'to see/visit', and آن رزمگاه is the definite object 'that battlefield'. The verb + به + noun phrase expresses motion/tendency toward seeing the place.
segments.s375-g37: همی indicates repeated/continuous action 'kept/was still'; گرد = 'around/about'; آن کشتگان = 'those slain'; بر بگشت (بگشت = past of برگشتن) means 'turned/returned/turned about'. Together it means he kept going about the corpses.
segments.s375-g38: کرا is an archaic relative/pronominal 'whom/which' referring back to the slain; دید = 'he saw' (past); بگریست = 'he wept' (prefixed ب- common in older Persian); و = 'and'; اندر گذشت = 'he passed on/thereupon passed' (اندر here 'thereupon/in that place' and گذشت = past 'passed').
segments.s375-g39: برادرش = 'his brother' (برادر + possessive ش); را is the definite object marker marking برادرش as the object; دید is 'he saw'. کشته is a past participle 'slain'; به زار is a predicative/adjectival phrase 'in a wretched/pitiable state' describing the brother.
segments.s375-g40: به آوردگاهی is a locative phrase 'on/at the battlefield' (به = onto/toward, آوردگاهی = place of bringing/battlefield). برافگنده is an adjectival participle meaning 'spread out/laid low' (describing how the body lies), and خوار is a postpositive adjective 'despised/abject'. The sequence gives the location and humiliating condition of the corpse.
segments.s375-g9: بدین اندر آییم و خواهش کنیم is a pair of jussive/subjunctive actions spoken by the speaker (first person plural). بدین (ب + دین here = “by/with this” or “thus”) + اندر (“into/among”) sets the scene adverbially: “enter among them in this way.” آییم is the classical subjunctive/imperative-like first-person-plural of آمدن (“let us come/enter”). و links the two verbs; خواهش کنیم is another present-subjunctive first-person-plural meaning “let us request/beg.” Together they form coordinated jussives: “let us enter…and let us beg.”
segments.s375-g10: همه آذران را نیایش کنیم continues the jussive mood: نیایش کنیم is the first-person-plural subjunctive of کردن used with the noun نیایش (“prayer, supplication”) to mean “let us pray/entreat.” همه آذْران را is the definite direct object (را marks it): “all the Āzarān / all the people called Āzarān” (or “all the fires” in some poetics, but here it functions as a people-group). Word order is typical Persian: object (with همه and را) before the verb.
segments.s375-g11: ازیشان چو بشنید اسفندیار uses a subordinating temporal clause. از ایشان (ازیشان) = “of them / concerning them” (shows what was heard). چو is the classical particle meaning “when.” بشنید is a preterite (classical past) form meaning “he heard.” The personal subject (اسفندیار) comes at the end in typical classical order: “When Esfandiyār heard of them….”
segments.s375-g12: به جان و به تن دادشان زینهار is a compact clause where به جان و به تن is an idiomatic intensifier (“by [his] life and by [his] body,” i.e., a solemn or earnest manner). دادشان is داد (past “gave”) with the third-person plural/clitic object -شان (“gave them”). زینهار functions as the thing given — here a noun “a warning/admonition” (rather than the interjectional ‘beware’). So: “he gave them zinehār (a warning), earnestly/by his life and body.”
segments.s375-g13: بران لشگر گشن آواز داد begins with بران (bar + آن in older usage) meaning roughly “to/against those” or “upon them/thereon” and locates the action. لشگر is the subject “the army/host.” گشن here is an attributive adjective or descriptive word qualifying the cry (in classical diction it colors the shout; the basic verb phrase is آواز داد “gave a cry/sang/shouted”), and آواز داد is a common compound meaning “uttered a voice/call” (داد is the past ‘gave’ used with آواز to mean ‘uttered’). Word order places modifiers before the verb as is typical.
segments.s375-g14: گو نامبردار فرخ‌نژاد presents the content of the cry. گو is a quotative/participial use meaning “saying” or the imperative/participial “call!”; it introduces the words shouted. نامبردار is an epithet “the famed/afore‑named” (an epithet used before a name). فرخ‌نژاد is the proper name being shouted. Together: “shouting, ‘the famed Farrokh‑nezhād’.”
segments.s375-g15: که این نامداران ایرانیان is a nominal phrase introduced by که (here a complementizer/relative particle marking reported speech or content: “that / who”). این (“this/the foregoing”) points to the group; نامداران is a plural adjective/noun “the famed/renowned ones.” ایرانیان specifies their identity: “the Iranians.” Together they form the noun phrase “that these famed Iranians ….”
segments.s375-g16: بگردید زین لشکر چینیان contains the verb بگردید (classical form of گشتن/گردیدن) used here in a past/perfective or result sense “were turned / became / were made to turn / were surrounded.” زین + لشکر = “this host/army” (زین is an older demonstrative/relational element here) and چینیان identifies them: “the Chinese.” The verb therefore expresses a change of state or being turned/encircled with the agent phrase “by this Chinese army.” Word order places the verb before the agent phrase in this elevated/classical style.
segments.s375-g17: کنون = temporal adverb 'now' placed before the clause. کاین سپاهِ عدو = a noun phrase 'this/that host of the enemy' where کاین (this/that) pre-modifies سپاه (host, army) and عدو (enemy) qualifies it. گشت پست = گشت (past of گشتن) is the stative/resultative 'became/has become' and is followed by the predicate adjective پست ('low/humbled, despicable'); together they mean 'has become low/humbled'.
segments.s375-g18: ازین سهم و کشتن = a prepositional phrase headed by از ('from/of') + این (spelled ین here) + سهم ('share, spoil') coordinated with the verbal noun کشتن ('killing'); بدارید دست is an imperative (2pl) construction literally 'keep/hold the hand' used idiomatically to mean 'refrain/hold back'. Grammatically, بدارید takes the following از‑phrase as its object of prevention.
segments.s375-g19: که begins a causal/explanatory clause ('for/because'). بس is an intensifier here ('very/so'). زاروارند is a predicative form meaning 'they are wretched/miserable' (زار‑ + ‑وارند), and بیچاره‌وار is an adjectival/adiverbial phrase 'in a pitiable manner' or 'pitiable', coordinated by و.
segments.s375-g20: دهید is an imperative (2pl) of دادن 'give' used here in the sense 'grant/spare' or 'do to them'; این سگان را is the direct object 'these dogs/scoundrels' (را marks the definite object). به جان زینهار is a formulaic exclamation: به جان 'by (my) life' as an oath/emphasis, and زینهار 'beware!/take heed!' functions as an admonitory tag following the plea.
segments.s375-g21: بدارید دست = imperative 'hold back (your) hand' (idiomatic: desist). از گرفتن = 'from taking/seizing' (گرفتن here = 'to capture/take'), and کنون is the temporal adverb 'now' placed at the end; the prepositional phrase from از governs the verbal noun گرفتن as the thing to refrain from.
segments.s375-g22: مبندید is a negative imperative form (here 'do not bind/tie/close'); کس را = 'anyone' as the object (را marks it); مریزید خون = negative imperative 'do not shed/spill blood' (م‑ prefix signals prohibition on the verb مریزید). The two imperatives are coordinated to forbid binding or killing.
segments.s375-g23: متازید is an imperative (2pl) from تازیدن 'rush/charge' meaning 'hurry/charge!'. و links the clauses. این کشتگان is the object 'these slain' and مسپرید is an imperative from سپردن/پرید compound meaning here 'send off/throw away/scatter' the slain — i.e. dispose of or scatter the bodies.
segments.s375-g24: بگردید is imperative (2pl) of گشتن meaning 'go around/roam/turn about'. و links to the final command: این خستگان (these exhausted/weary ones) is the object and بشمرید is the imperative of شمردن 'count/number' (i.e. go and take count of them).
segments.s375-g25: 'مگیریدشان' is the verbal phrase with the old-present prefix (م- / می-) plus the verb 'گیرید' and the clitic 'شان' (them), here rendered as 'they seized/took them'. 'بهر' is the preposition 'for/for the sake of' and 'جان زریر' means 'the life of Zarrir' (جان = life, زریر = proper name); the whole segment expresses purpose/reason: they took them for Zarrir's life.
segments.s375-g26: Word order is locative/adverbial + verb + temporal adjective: 'بر اسپان جنگی' = 'upon/at the war-horses' (اسپان = horses, جنگی = warlike/war-horses). 'مپایید' is the present/habitual marker + verb 'پاییدن' (to lie in wait), here past/narrative sense 'lay in wait' and 'دیر' (= long) is a temporal adverb modifying the waiting.
segments.s375-g27: 'چو' = 'when' introducing a temporal clause. 'لشکر شنیدند آواز اوی' is literally 'the army heard his voice' with past 3rd-plural verb 'شنیدند'. 'آواز اوی' uses the old possessive 'اوی' after the noun, i.e. 'his voice'.
segments.s375-g28: 'شدند' is the past 3rd-plural of 'شدن' used here as 'they rose/appeared/set out'. The small prepositional phrase 'از بر' = 'from beside/out of' governs 'خستگان' (the weary). 'بارزوی' is an adverbial ('with desire' / 'eagerly') modifying the action, so the sequence means they got up/moved away from the exhausted ones in eager expectation.
segments.s375-g29: 'به لشکرگه خود فرود آمدند' is straightforward: 'به' marks direction ('to/into'), 'لشکرگه' = 'army camp', 'خود' = 'their own', and 'فرود آمدند' is past 3rd-plural 'they came down/descended' (i.e., returned to their camp).
segments.s375-g30: The phrase begins with 'به' marking purpose: 'به پیروز گشتن' = 'in order to become victorious' or 'to secure victory' ('گشتن' = to become). 'تبیره زدند' has 'زدند' (they struck/they uttered) with 'تبیره' as its object; in this poetic context it functions as 'a proclamation/exclamation' (hence 'they uttered a proclamation to ensure victory'). Grammatically, this is a purposive clause followed by the action taken.
segments.s375-g31: 'همه شب' ('all night') is an adverbial temporal phrase. 'نخفتند' is past-negative 'they did not sleep' (ن- + خفتند). 'زان خرمی' = 'from that joy'/'because of that delight' (زان = from that/for that reason; خرمی = joy/delight), giving the cause for their sleeplessness.
segments.s375-g32: 'که' introduces an explanatory clause: 'پیروزی بودشان رستمی' = 'the victory was theirs/Rostam's'. 'پیروزی' = victory, 'بودشان' is past 'it was to them/they had it' (بود + enclitic -شان), and 'رستمی' qualifies as 'of Rostam' (possession/association). The clause explains the reason for the preceding joy.
segments.s375-g1: چو is the subordinating conjunction 'when' (archaic/poetic). ترکان is the plural subject 'the Turks'. بدیدند is a past tense verb 'they saw' (3rd plural). The following clause کارجاسپ رفت is the main/result clause: a proper name (کارجاسپ) + past verb رفت 'went/fled'. The sequence reflects classical Persian clause linkage: چو + subject + verb (protasis), then the main clause.
segments.s375-g2: همی is an archaic/progressive particle that combines with a present-tense verb to mark ongoing or habitual action; here it gives a continuous sense to آید ('was coming/came'). از هر سوی is a prepositional phrase 'from every direction'; تیغ تفت is the nominal phrase 'sword(s) [with tift]', where تیغ is 'sword' and تفت is poetic/qualifying (here used metaphorically). Word order places the adverbial/locative phrase before the verb in classical style.
segments.s375-g3: همه is the quantifier 'all/everyone' placed before the noun. سرکشانشان is a possessed plural noun: سرکشان (rebels/proud ones) + possessive suffix -شان 'their'. پیاده شد(ند) is the verbal phrase meaning 'became on foot' → 'dismounted' or 'got off (their horses)'; شدند is the past-tense plural of شدن used as the main verb.
segments.s375-g4: به is the preposition 'to/toward' introducing the indirect object. پیش‌گو is a compound noun 'foreteller/soothsayer' (here 'seer'), which names the person they approach; اسفندیار is that person's name. آمدند is the past plural verb 'they came'. The structure is preposition + object + verb: 'they came to the seer Esfandiyar.'
segments.s375-g5: This clause lists two items (likely proper names or objects) followed by the past transitive verb بینداختند 'they threw/cast down'. In classical Persian a sequence of nouns before a verb often marks a compound object. بی- انداز (بینداختند) is the prefixed past form meaning 'flung' or 'cast aside'.
segments.s375-g6: قبای نبردی is a noun phrase 'battle-robe(s)' (قبای = robe(s); نبردی = 'of battle' / martial). برون is the adverb 'out/forth' placed before the verb. آختند here is an archaic/presentational past plural verb meaning roughly 'they leapt/they went forth / they put on and went out' in context; the clause describes gearing for battle and issuing forth. Poetic compression leaves precise voice somewhat elliptic.
segments.s375-g7: به زاریش is an adverbial phrase 'with lamentation / in entreaty' (به + زاریش). گفتند‌گر is a fused narrative formula: گفتند (they said) + گر (if) introducing a conditional quotation. شهریار is the subject of the conditional clause ('the king'). Together this introduces reported speech beginning with a conditional protasis.
segments.s375-g8: دهد is the 3rd-person present subjunctive/indicative 'gives' used in the protasis 'if the king gives'. بندگان را is the direct object 'the servants' with the object-marker را. به جان is an oath-like prepositional phrase 'by (my/your) life'; زینهار is an interjection 'beware / take heed' functioning as a warning. Together the clause expresses a pleading warning: 'If the king hands over the servants—by my life, beware!'
segments.s375-g41: چو is a temporal conjunction 'when' introducing the clause; او را is the definite object ('him') marked by the object marker را and appears before the verb (typical Persian SOV order). چنان زار و کشته is a string of predicative adjectives ('so/thus' + 'wretched' + 'slain') describing او; بدید is the past tense verb 'saw' placed at the end of the clause.
segments.s375-g42: همه functions as an indefinite plural subject 'everyone' (understood plural); جامه خسروی is a noun phrase 'royal garments' where خسروی adjectivally qualifies جامه and follows it (noun + adjective is common in classical Persian). بردرید is the past tense verb 'tore apart/tore off' placed finally; the clause follows the SOV ordering with the agent implied by همه.
segments.s375-g43: فرود آمد is a past-tense verbal phrase 'came down / took down' here describing the action of removing or letting down something; از + noun (از شولک) indicates the source or thing affected ('from/off the shulak'). شولک خوب رنگ is a compound noun phrase 'a shulak of good/beautiful color' (شولک = small cloak, خوب رنگ = finely/beautifully colored).
segments.s375-g44: This is a verb–object cluster describing an act on the beard: به ریش خود = 'to/at his own beard' (به + noun + possessive خود). اندر زده is a compound-style past participle/verb phrase 'struck/put into' (اندر = into/in; زده = struck/has struck). هر دو چنگ follows and specifies instrument/means 'with both hands' (چنگ in poetry can mean hand/claw); word order is somewhat inverted for emphasis but the meaning is 'he struck/tore at his beard with both hands.'
segments.s375-g45: همی گفت is an iterative past expression 'he kept saying / repeatedly said' (همی marks continuous or habitual action). کی is the interrogative 'who' (here introducing an emphatic question about identity). شاه گردان بلخ is a nominal phrase 'the king/commander of the troop of Balkh' (شاه modified by گردان, with بلخ as place/genitive). The clause order places the interrogative before the noun phrase being questioned; گفت functions as the reporting verb for the quoted speech.
segments.s375-g46: همه زندگانی ما is the object phrase 'all our lives' (همه = all; زندگانی = life/livelihood; ما = our). کرده + تلخ is a light/resultative construction: کرده ('has made') plus the adjective تلخ ('bitter') yields 'has made (them) bitter.' In classical Persian the object often precedes the verbal predicate (here 'همه زندگانی ما' before 'کرده تلخ').
segments.s375-g47: دریغا is an exclamatory lament 'Alas! / what a pity'. سوارا (with the -ا ending marking the object/accusative) names the affected party 'the horsemen/riders'. شها is a vocative 'O king!' inserted to address the sovereign. خسروا here is used as a verbal idea 'they perished / were ruined' (past), completing the lament about the riders.
segments.s375-g48: نبرده is a negative perfect/past form 'has not taken / did not capture'. دلیرا is the accusative or vocative 'the brave one' (دلیرا = brave); گزیده is an adjectival or appositive 'chosen/selected' qualifying that brave one. گوا is a particle meaning 'apparently / it seems / as witness' used here to hedge the statement. Together the cluster states that (they) have not taken the brave, the chosen one, apparently.
segments.s375-g57: 'بفرمود' is the honorific past verb meaning 'he ordered/said' and introduces a subordinate purpose/command clause with 'تا'. 'تا' here = 'so that / in order that' and governs the following verb. 'کشتگان' is a plural noun 'the slain', and 'بشمرند' is a 3rd-person plural subjunctive/imperative used after 'تا' to express the commanded action 'to count'.
segments.s375-g58: This clause is the second coordinated command: 'کسی را' (object marked by 'را') = 'someone' (the direct object). 'که' is a relative pronoun 'who', introducing 'خسته‌ست' (present copula 'is tired'). The verb 'بیرون برند' is 3rd-person plural subjunctive/imperative ('they should carry/take out'), so the object precedes the verb in standard Persian order with 'را' marking it.
segments.s375-g59: 'بگردید' is a 2nd-person-plural imperative/subjunctive from گشتن with the prefix 'ب-' meaning 'go about/roam/search'. The phrase 'بر گرد' (= 'around') is formed by the preposition 'بر' + noun 'گرد' and takes the demonstrative 'آن' ('that') before the location noun 'رزمگاه' ('battlefield'), so the whole means 'go around that battlefield'.
segments.s375-g60: This is a coordinated list of places introduced by the preposition 'به' ('to/into/towards') for the first items and 'بر' ('on/upon') repeated before 'دشت' for parallelism. It enumerates locations to be searched: 'کوه' (mountain), 'بیابان' (wilderness), 'دشت' (plain) and 'راه' (road). The conjunction 'و' simply links the items.
segments.s375-g61: The phrase begins with the genitive preposition 'از' ('of/from'), so 'از ایرانیان' = 'of the Iranians'. 'کشته' is a past participle used predicatively ('killed'), followed by the adverb 'بد' (here an archaic/poetic intensifier roughly 'badly/indeed'), and the numeral 'سی‌هزار' ('thirty thousand') gives the number of those killed. Word order is the older Persian pattern 'Of X killed were N.'
segments.s375-g62: 'ازان' = 'of those / of them' refers back to the previously mentioned group. It introduces the smaller group quantified by the following numeral (here omitted in the word list but present in the text). 'سرکش و نامدار' are two coordinated adjectives: 'سرکش' = 'rebellious/wild' and 'نامدار' = 'renowned/illustrious', describing that subset.
segments.s375-g63: The coordinating 'و' links this clause to the previous. 'هزار و چل' is a compound numeral 'a thousand and forty' (poetic word order). 'از نامور' = 'of the famed/renowned', and 'خسته بود' is the past-tense copula 'was exhausted/was tired' (بود = past 'was'), describing that group.
segments.s375-g64: This is a relative clause introduced by 'که' ('who/which'). 'از پای پیلان' literally 'from the feet of the elephants' (پایل = 'foot', پیلان = plural of 'پیل' = 'elephant'), and 'به در جسته بود' is a compound past: 'جسته بود' (pluperfect/past perfect 'had leapt/sprung/been thrown') with 'به در' ('to the opening / out') — together meaning 'had been thrown away/cleared out (from under the elephants)' or 'had leapt free from beneath the elephants.'
segments.s375-g49: A string of nominative epithets without an explicit copula: ستون منا = “a pillar (ستون) of/for us (منا)”, پرده کشورا = “a veil/cover (پرده) of the country/realm (کشورا)”. Poetic Persian often omits the ezāfe (ِ) and uses the enclitic منا to give a possessive/partitive sense (“for/of us”). Word order is noun + modifier (possessive follows the noun).
segments.s375-g50: Continues the appositive list: چراغ جهان = “the lamp/light (چراغ) of the world (جهان)”; افسر لشکرا = an epithet 'the officer/commander (افسر) of the army (لشکرا)'. Again there is no copula; nouns and their genitives are juxtaposed in classical/poetic order.
segments.s375-g51: Two past actions joined by و: فرود آمد (third‑person past) = “he descended/ alighted”; برگرفتش = برگرفت + -ش (object clitic) = “he took him up / lifted him”. ز خاک is a simple ablative 'from the ground'. The verb forms are past tense, third person singular.
segments.s375-g52: Instrumental/agent phrase + object + verb: به دست خودش = “by/with his own hand” (به + noun + possessive خودش), روی = “the face” as direct object, بسترد = past of ستر (بسترد) = “he covered/veiled”. پاک here functions adverbially = “completely/entirely.” The subject is implied from previous clause (third‑singular).
segments.s375-g53: به تابوت زرینش اندر نهاد = be + noun + adjective+possessive + اندر (in) + نهاد (past of نهادن ‘to place’). Here به … اندر … نهاد = “he placed into …”. زرینش is زرین + -ش (possessive), ‘his golden [coffin]’; the verb نهاد is past 3rd singular.
segments.s375-g54: تو گفتی = second‑person past 'you said' (introducing reported speech). The reported clause uses زریر (proper name) + از بنه خود (از = 'from', بنه خود = 'his own stock/line' with خود emphatic) + نزاد (archaic past form meaning 'was born' / 'sprang'). Note: some lemmas in the provided list do not match the actual grammatical use (see senses).
segments.s375-g55: A plural subject phrase: کیان زادگان = 'the royal/kings' offspring' (کیان = royal/kings, زادگان = born/offspring), و جوانان خویش = 'and his young men/youths' (جوانان = youths, خوی + ش = 'his own'). This nominative phrase is the actor of the following plural verb.
segments.s375-g56: به تابوتها در نهادند پیش = they (plural) placed (نهادند) into the coffins (به تابوتها; تابوتها is plural) with در functioning as 'in/inside' and پیش adding the sense 'before/in the presence of' (i.e. placed them into the coffins before [someone]). نهادند is past 3rd plural agreeing with the plural subject.

sentences.s375-s33: And of those others, one hundred thousand and one hundred and sixty‑three renowned ones were slain.
sentences.s375-s34: From the exhausted (foes) three thousand and two hundred remained on that spot — as many as strength allowed.
sentences.s375-s17: When, in the rout of that dark night, blood poured down into the plains and the wilderness.
sentences.s375-s18: The famed Ki came with the chiefs of the army to behold that battlefield.
sentences.s375-s19: He turned again about those slain; when he saw them he wept and passed on.
sentences.s375-s20: He saw his brother slain and pitiable, cast upon the battlefield, despised.
sentences.s375-s5: Let us enter among them like this and make a petition; let us entreat all the Āzarān with prayer.
sentences.s375-s6: When Esfandiyār heard of them, he gave them a solemn warning, by life and by body.
sentences.s375-s7: At that the army uttered a cry; calling out, “Renowned Farrokh‑nezhād!”
sentences.s375-s8: saying that these famed Iranians were turned/encircled by this Chinese host.
sentences.s375-s9: Now that this enemy host has been humbled, hold back your hand from this spoil and from killing.
sentences.s375-s10: For they are very miserable and pitiable; spare these dogs — by my life, take heed!
sentences.s375-s11: Hold back your hand from taking (them) now; do not bind anyone, do not spill blood.
sentences.s375-s12: Rush, and cast away these slain; go about and count these exhausted ones.
sentences.s375-s13: They seized them for Zarrir's life; on war-horses they lay in wait for a long time.
sentences.s375-s14: When the army heard his voice, they rose from beside the weary, eager.
sentences.s375-s15: They descended to their own camp; to secure victory they uttered a proclamation.
sentences.s375-s16: All night they did not sleep from that delight, for the victory was Rostam's.
sentences.s375-s1: When the Turks saw it, Karjasp went off; swords came from every direction.
sentences.s375-s2: All their rebels dismounted on foot; they came to the seer Esfandiyar.
sentences.s375-s3: They hurled down Kamančāy and Chachi; they put on (or threw out) battle-robes and went forth.
sentences.s375-s4: They pleaded, saying, 'If the king hands over the servants, by my life—beware!'
sentences.s375-s21: When he saw him so wretched and slain, everyone tore the royal garments.
sentences.s375-s22: He took down (tore off) the finely colored shulak and thrust both hands into his own beard.
sentences.s375-s23: He kept saying, 'Who is the king — the commander of the troops of Balkh — who has made all our lives bitter?'.
sentences.s375-s24: Alas! The horsemen — O king — have perished; they have not taken the brave, the chosen one, it seems.
sentences.s375-s29: He commanded that they count the slain, and that they carry out anyone who is exhausted.
sentences.s375-s30: Search around that battlefield — into the mountains and the wilderness, and upon the plain and the roads.
sentences.s375-s31: Of the Iranians thirty thousand were killed; of them seven hundred were rebellious and renowned.
sentences.s375-s32: And a thousand and forty of the famed were exhausted, who had been flung from (under) the elephants' feet.
sentences.s375-s25: A pillar for us, a veil for the realm, the light of the world, the commander of the army.
sentences.s375-s26: He descended and lifted him from the earth; with his own hand he completely covered his face.
sentences.s375-s27: He placed him in his golden coffin; you said Zarir was born of his own stock.
sentences.s375-s28: The royal-born and his young men placed them in the coffins before him.
